title: Adaptive Opponent Modeling for Adversarial Co-Training in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
description: A controlled study of uncertainty-aware opponent modeling in a hidden-intent predator–prey game (JAX/JaxMARL). A belief over the opponent's latent strategy is inferred from its motion and drives a belief-conditioned Monte-Carlo planner (+61% captures over an opponent-blind baseline, above an oracle handed the true strategy); a confident-but-wrong point estimate is worse than no opponent model at all (−47%). A predictive (JEPA) opponent encoder beats the VAE-for-opponent-modeling baseline (probe 0.88 vs 0.54), reads the strategy from roughly half the observation steps, and yields a label-free belief that matches the supervised pipeline — with an honest negative result for JEPA as a planning world model. Includes the full paper, literature review, reproducible experiments (the mopa package), figures, and demo GIFs.
